mappings.rationaleAI: Covers the An Lushan rebellion (755–763) and its aftermath, provincial separatism, Tang cultural life including Buddhist influences and visual arts, and social-economic developments — mapping precisely to A.1.a, A.1.b, and A.1.c of the late Tang period.

Under Yuan (Mongol) rule the Silk Road expanded greatly as Mongols promoted trade; under Ming, foreign economic relations included maritime trade — relevant to B.1.b (expansion of trade under Mongols) and C.1.b (Ming foreign relations and economic policy).

The Yuan dynasty section of section 932 is entirely about the Mongol Empire's China chapter; the article covers Kublai Khan's conquest of the Song, the transfer of the capital to Ta-tu (Peking), Mongol administration, religious toleration, and Yuan China's contacts with the West.

Covers Tang and Song dynasty China as central examples of post-classical civilization alongside the Mongol Empire's role in connecting Eurasia, directly relevant to the A (Tang/Song) and B (Yuan) sections of the outline.
The China overview article spans Tang, Song, Yuan, Ming, and Qing dynastic history with sufficient detail to provide broad coverage of all three major sections of the 932 outline.

Genghis Khan's conquest of the Jin (Chin) dynasty in northern China (1211–34) is the foundational event of B.1.a, and his military organization and restructuring of Mongol society are essential context for understanding the Mongol conquest of China.

The Sung period saw the resurgence of Neo-Confucianism (explicitly named at A.3); Confucius's article explains the foundational philosophy that Neo-Confucian thinkers were systematizing, providing necessary intellectual context.

The Islamic Golden Age overlaps chronologically with Yuan China's contacts with the West, and the Mongol connections between the Islamic world and Yuan China — including the arrival of Catholic missionaries and commercial contacts — are covered in B.2.b.

Beijing (Ta-tu/Peking) was the Mongol capital under Kublai Khan (B.1.b) and remained the imperial capital through Ming (C.1) and Qing (C.2) dynasties, making the article's historical material on Beijing's role directly relevant.

Covers Ming, Qing, Joseon, Mongol Empire, and Silk Road trade within a single Asian history narrative, providing broad background context spanning all three major dynastic sections of the 932 outline.

The Black Death originated in Central Asia and spread along Mongol trade networks during the Yuan dynasty period — directly relevant to the Mongol Empire's role in B.1 (Mongol conquest and administration) and B.2 (decline of Mongol rule).

Covers Chinese dynastic civilization from Tang through Qing as the dominant regional cultural tradition, along with tributary relations with Korea and Japan, providing broad overview context for the full chronological scope of section 932.
Expansion of commerce is explicitly referenced at A.1.c (Tang), B.1.b (Mongol expansion of trade), C.1.b (Ming foreign economic policy), and C.2.b (Ch'ing expansion of industry and commerce).

The article covers the introduction of new crops and spread of rice cultivation globally; C.2.i specifically addresses advances in agriculture through increased rice cultivation and introduction of new crops during mid-Ch'ing.

The general agriculture article covers crop systems and agricultural development including in China, relevant to C.2.i's focus on agricultural advances and new crop introductions during the mid-Ch'ing period.
